section: Chapter 12: Magnetic Effects of Electric Current

concepts.lesson: When electric current flows through a conductor, it produces a magnetic field around it. This phenomenon is known as the magnetic effect of electric current.
concepts.real_example: A compass needle placed near a current-carrying wire deflects due to the magnetic field produced by the current.

concepts.lesson: Magnetic field lines represent the direction and strength of a magnetic field. They form closed curves and never intersect each other.
concepts.real_example: Iron filings sprinkled around a magnet form patterns that show the magnetic field lines.

concepts.lesson: The right-hand thumb rule helps determine the direction of magnetic field around a straight current-carrying conductor. If the thumb points in the direction of current, the curled fingers show the direction of the magnetic field.
concepts.real_example: Using the rule to predict the circular magnetic field around a vertical current-carrying wire.
key_points: Fingers show direction of magnetic field

concepts.concept_name: Magnetic Field Due to a Solenoid
concepts.lesson: A solenoid is a long coil of insulated wire. When current flows through it, it produces a magnetic field similar to that of a bar magnet with distinct north and south poles.
concepts.real_example: A solenoid connected to a battery acts like a magnet and can attract iron objects.

concepts.lesson: An electromagnet is a temporary magnet produced when electric current flows through a coil wound around a soft iron core. It loses magnetism when the current stops.
concepts.real_example: Electromagnets are used in cranes to lift heavy iron scrap in factories.

concepts.lesson: When a current-carrying conductor is placed in a magnetic field, it experiences a force. The direction of this force depends on the direction of current and the magnetic field.
concepts.real_example: A wire carrying current placed between two magnetic poles moves due to magnetic force.
key_points: Depends on direction of current and field

concepts.lesson: Fleming's left-hand rule determines the direction of force on a current-carrying conductor placed in a magnetic field. The thumb, forefinger and middle finger of the left hand are kept perpendicular to each other.
concepts.real_example: Engineers use this rule to determine the direction of motion in electric motors.

concepts.lesson: An electric motor is a device that converts electrical energy into mechanical energy using the interaction between magnetic fields and electric current.
concepts.real_example: Electric fans, mixers and washing machines use electric motors.
key_points: Converts electrical energy to mechanical energy
Uses magnetic force on current-carrying coil
Contains coil, magnet, split ring and brushes

concepts.lesson: Electromagnetic induction is the process of generating electric current in a conductor by changing the magnetic field around it.
concepts.real_example: Moving a magnet inside a coil connected to a galvanometer produces current in the coil.
key_points: Change in magnetic field induces current

concepts.lesson: An electric generator converts mechanical energy into electrical energy using electromagnetic induction. Electrical safety devices such as fuses and earthing protect circuits and appliances from damage.
concepts.real_example: Hydroelectric power plants use generators to produce electricity, while fuses protect household appliances from excessive current.
key_points: Generator converts mechanical energy to electrical energy
Fuse and earthing ensure electrical safety
